summary:
In the aftermath of the [[event:2690|Tiananmen Square crackdown]] of June 4, 1989, the Chinese Communist Party under Deng Xiaoping moved decisively to consolidate political control while reaffirming commitment to economic reform. Martial law remained in effect in Beijing until January 1990. Thousands of students and intellectuals were arrested; some were tried and executed, while others received lengthy prison sentences. Zhao Ziyang, the party general secretary who had expressed sympathy with the protesters, was placed under house arrest where he remained until his death in 2005.

The immediate international reaction was severe: Western countries imposed economic sanctions and arms embargoes, the World Bank suspended loans to China, and Chinese leaders were diplomatically isolated. However, these pressures proved short-lived. The collapse of the Soviet Union and the demonstration that communist party rule need not follow the Soviet path reinforced the CCP's confidence in its hybrid model of economic openness with political authoritarianism. By 1992, Deng Xiaoping's southern inspection tour revived reform momentum, and China's economy resumed rapid growth. The June Fourth Incident, as it is euphemistically known within China, became one of the most censored and suppressed events in Chinese contemporary history.
